title: Sloppy Joe
subtitle: Sweet and spiced ground beef in tomato sauce on a soft bun
excerpt: American diner classic: beef mince cooked down with onion, pepper, ketchup, and a little brown sugar into a loose, saucy filling that tumbles over the sides of a toasted brioche bun. Quick to make, impossible to eat neatly.
sourceNotes: The Sloppy Joe became widespread in American school cafeteria and diner culture in the mid-twentieth century, with origins traced to a Sioux City, Iowa cook named Joe who served loose meat sandwiches in the 1930s, and to the loose-meat sandwich tradition documented in Midwestern American cooking from the 1910s onward. Similar loose beef preparations appear in American domestic cookery from Fannie Farmer's 'The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book' (1896, Project Gutenberg #7544).

recipe.freezeNotes: The meat sauce freezes well for up to 3 months. Defrost overnight in the fridge and reheat gently on the hob, adding a splash of water if it has thickened.
recipe.batchNotes: Double or triple the sauce and freeze in portions. Toast buns fresh each time.

content.text: The Sloppy Joe is not trying to be elegant. The name is accurate. The filling (beef mince, tomato, a little sweetness, some vinegar for sharpness) is cooked until properly loose and saucy, then spooned onto a soft bun so generously that it runs over the edges and onto the plate. This is the intended outcome. Children love it for the same reason adults find it embarrassing in company: it is food that requires your full attention.

The sauce needs about 15 minutes of cooking to reduce to the right consistency: thick enough to mound on the bun without pooling on the plate, loose enough that the first bite makes a mess. The ratio of ketchup to Worcestershire to brown sugar produces a sweet-sharp balance that is distinctly American. Add a pinch of chilli powder if you want heat. Use a 20 per cent fat mince for the best texture.

items.prepNote: brioche buns or soft white rolls, split and toasted

content.text: Brown the mince and soften the vegetables

Set the pan over medium-high heat. Add the {{beef-mince}} of beef mince to the dry pan and cook for 5 to 6 minutes, breaking up with a spoon, until browned and any liquid has evaporated. Push the mince to one side, add the {{onion}} diced onion and {{pepper-green}} diced green pepper to the cleared space, and cook for 4 to 5 minutes until softened. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and stir everything together.

Add the {{ketchup}} of ketchup, {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée, {{worcestershire-sauce}} of Worcestershire sauce, {{dark-brown-sugar}} of dark brown sugar, {{cider-vinegar}} of cider vinegar, chilli powder if using, and a pinch of salt. Stir well and add 100 ml of water. Bring to a simmer and cook for 10 to 12 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sauce has thickened to a loose but not runny consistency. Taste and adjust for salt, sweetness, or acidity.

Toast the split buns in a dry pan or under the grill until lightly golden. Spoon the meat sauce generously onto the bottom half — it should overflow slightly. Top with the bun lid and serve immediately with a stack of paper napkins.

items.symptom: Sauce is watery and does not hold on the bun
items.fix: Continue simmering uncovered until the sauce has a thick, spoonable consistency; it should mound slightly when piled onto the bun

items.cause: Too much ketchup or brown sugar relative to vinegar
items.fix: Add the cider vinegar a little at a time, tasting as you go; a splash of Worcestershire sauce also balances the sweetness
Use the back of a wooden spoon to break the mince into small pieces from the moment it goes into the pan; large clumps make the texture uneven

items.cause: Bun not toasted, or too much sauce applied
items.fix: Toast the bun until it has a golden crust on the cut face; this creates a brief barrier between the bread and the sauce

content.text: The Sloppy Joe belongs to the American school-cafeteria and diner tradition, a dish designed to be made in large quantities, served fast, and eaten without ceremony. The name became attached to the loose-meat sandwich in the 1930s and 1940s, and by the 1950s it was on menus and in home-cooking repertoires across the country. The tinned version (Manwich) launched in 1969 and sold the dish to a generation of households as a weeknight shortcut. The homemade version takes twenty minutes and tastes better, but the tinned version made the Sloppy Joe a national institution.
